study_guide_title: Comprehensive Solved Past Questions Study Guide (Corrected & Grounded)

questions.question: In Java, a class is defined using which keyword?
questions.explanation: The 'class' keyword is the fundamental reserved word used to declare a blueprint for objects in Java.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to instantiate a new object in Java?
questions.explanation: The 'new' operator allocates memory on the heap and returns a reference to the newly created object.
questions.code:
// Instantiating an object with the "new" keyword
MyClass obj = new MyClass();

questions.question: What is the primary role of a constructor?
questions.explanation: Constructors are special methods invoked during object creation to set initial values for fields.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT one of the four main pillars of OOP?
questions.explanation: Compilation is a language processing step; the pillars are Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction.

questions.question: What does a constructor return?

options.c: Nothing, it has no return type

questions.explanation: Constructors have no return type, not even void. They implicitly return the instance of the class being created.

questions.question: A variable that belongs to the class rather than an instance is declared as:
questions.explanation: The 'static' keyword indicates that the member belongs to the class, shared by all instances.

questions.question: Which keyword prevents a class from being inherited?
questions.explanation: Declaring a class as 'final' seals it, preventing any subclassing. This is a common security practice.

questions.question: What is the result of 'this' keyword inside a constructor?

options.b: Refers to the current object instance

questions.explanation: 'this' is a reference to the current object being constructed or manipulated.

questions.question: Which of these is a valid way to overload a constructor?
questions.explanation: Overloading allows multiple constructors with different parameter signatures.

questions.question: What happens if you do not define a constructor in your class?

options.c: Java provides a default no-argument constructor
options.d: The object is created as null

questions.explanation: If no constructor is provided, Java automatically inserts a default public no-arg constructor.

questions.question: Which of these is a primitive type in Java?
questions.explanation: int is a primitive type; String and others are objects/classes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'main' method?

options.a: It is the only method allowed
options.b: It is the entry point for program execution

questions.explanation: The JVM looks for the signature `public static void main(String[] args)` to start execution.

questions.question: Java is often described as:
questions.explanation: Java is fundamentally an Object-Oriented Programming language.

questions.question: What does the JVM (Java Virtual Machine) do?
questions.explanation: The JVM interprets and executes compiled Java bytecode on various platforms.

questions.question: Which operator is used to check for reference equality?
questions.explanation: The '==' operator checks if two references point to the same memory location.

questions.question: What is the default value of an uninitialized int field in a class?
questions.explanation: Instance variables (fields) are initialized to default values (0 for int, false for boolean, null for objects).

questions.question: What is the root class of all classes in Java?
questions.explanation: Every class in Java implicitly extends java.lang.Object.

questions.question: Can you have multiple main methods in one class?

options.e: Yes, if they have different return types

questions.explanation: Yes, you can overload the main method, though only the standard signature is used by the JVM for entry.

questions.question: Which of these is a valid declaration of a constant?
questions.explanation: The 'final' keyword prevents modification of the variable once initialized.

questions.question: What is a 'package'?

options.c: A namespace for organizing related classes

questions.explanation: Packages provide structure and prevent naming conflicts in Java projects.

questions.question: Encapsulation is primarily used for:
questions.explanation: Encapsulation restricts direct access to data, protecting the internal state of an object.

questions.question: Which access modifier is the most restrictive?
questions.explanation: Private members are only accessible within the class they are defined in.

questions.question: What is a getter method?

options.b: A method to set a value
options.c: A method to retrieve the value of a private field

questions.explanation: Getters provide controlled, read-only or validated access to private fields.

questions.question: What is a setter method?

options.a: A method to update a private field
options.c: A method to initialize a class
options.d: A method to return a value

questions.explanation: Setters allow external classes to modify private fields safely, often with validation logic.

questions.question: Default access (no specifier) means the member is visible to:
questions.explanation: Also known as package-private, this is the default if no modifier is used.

questions.question: Which access modifier allows access from subclasses in different packages?
questions.explanation: Protected is visible to the package and subclasses elsewhere.

questions.question: Why use encapsulation?

options.a: To make code harder to hack
options.d: To remove the need for constructors
options.e: To allow public access to all variables

questions.explanation: By controlling access, developers can change internal implementations without breaking external code.

questions.question: Which keyword is NOT an access modifier?
questions.explanation: Final is a non-access modifier used to restrict entities (variables, methods, classes).

questions.question: Can a private method be accessed from a child class?

options.e: Yes, if in the same package

questions.explanation: Private members are strictly limited to the class in which they are defined.

questions.question: Access control in Java helps with:
questions.explanation: Access control allows developers to hide sensitive implementation logic from external tampering.

questions.question: What is data hiding?

options.c: Restricting access to internal object data

questions.explanation: Data hiding is the essence of encapsulation.

questions.question: If you need to strictly control validation when setting a variable, you should:
questions.explanation: Setters allow you to inject `if` checks or validation logic before assigning a value.

questions.question: Which modifier should be used for variables that are internal implementation details?
questions.explanation: Hiding implementation details prevents dependencies and accidental modification.

questions.question: Can a public class have private members?

options.e: No, if the class is public, all members must be public

questions.explanation: Access levels are defined per member, independent of the class modifier.

questions.question: Which modifier allows classes in other packages to access a member?
questions.explanation: Public is the least restrictive level.

questions.question: When does encapsulation make software safer?

options.c: When it adds a password to the class

questions.explanation: Encapsulation acts as a gatekeeper, ensuring valid state transitions.

questions.question: Which of these is considered a 'mutator' method?
questions.explanation: A mutator method modifies the internal state of an object.

questions.question: Which of these is considered an 'accessor' method?
questions.explanation: An accessor method provides read access to internal state.

questions.question: Can private members be accessed via reflection?
questions.explanation: Java Reflection API is a powerful feature that can bypass access modifiers for advanced manipulation.

questions.question: Is it good practice to make fields public?
questions.explanation: Public fields expose internal state to unintended changes, violating OOP best practices.

questions.question: Inheritance is achieved using which keyword?
questions.explanation: The 'extends' keyword is used to create a subclass in Java.

questions.question: Which of the following is true about inheritance?

options.a: It supports multiple inheritance of classes
options.b: It supports only single inheritance of classes
options.c: It is not supported in Java

questions.explanation: Java supports single inheritance of classes to avoid complexity like the 'diamond problem'.

questions.question: What is method overriding?

options.a: Creating methods with the same name and parameters in the same class
options.b: Redefining a superclass method in a subclass
options.d: Calling a method from another package

questions.explanation: Overriding provides specific subclass behavior for a method inherited from a parent.

questions.question: What is method overloading?

options.a: Multiple methods with same name but different parameters

questions.explanation: Overloading allows multiple methods to share a name if their parameter signatures are distinct.

questions.question: The 'super' keyword is used to:
questions.explanation: Super allows access to methods or fields of the parent class, or to call parent constructors.

questions.question: What is polymorphism?

options.a: The ability to exist in many forms
options.b: The ability to inherit many classes

questions.explanation: Polymorphism allows objects to be treated as instances of their superclass or interface.

questions.question: Which of these is compile-time polymorphism?
questions.explanation: Overloading is resolved at compile time based on parameter signatures.

questions.question: Which of these is runtime polymorphism?
questions.explanation: Overriding is resolved at runtime based on the actual object instance.

questions.question: Can you override a static method?
questions.explanation: Static methods are associated with the class, not instances, so they cannot be overridden (only hidden).

questions.question: What does a constructor chain start with?
questions.explanation: The first line of a constructor often calls another constructor (in the same class or superclass).

questions.question: The 'is-a' relationship describes:
questions.explanation: A subclass 'is a' superclass (e.g., a Car 'is a' Vehicle).

questions.question: What is the consequence of not overriding an abstract method in a non-abstract subclass?
questions.explanation: Concrete subclasses must implement all inherited abstract methods.

questions.question: Dynamic binding is associated with:
questions.explanation: Dynamic binding (late binding) links the method call to the implementation at runtime.

questions.question: Which of these demonstrates polymorphism?
questions.explanation: Using a parent reference (Vehicle) to hold a child object (Car) is a classic example of polymorphism.

questions.question: Multiple inheritance in Java is achieved via:
questions.explanation: Classes can implement multiple interfaces, allowing a form of multiple inheritance.

questions.question: Can you override a final method?
questions.explanation: The 'final' keyword forbids overriding.

questions.question: When does 'super' call the parent constructor?

options.a: Only at the beginning of the constructor
options.d: Only if the class is abstract

questions.explanation: super() must be the first statement in the constructor.

questions.question: Is inheritance mandatory in OOP?

options.e: Yes, for the code to compile

questions.explanation: You can design systems without inheritance using composition.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of code reuse?
questions.explanation: Inheritance allows subclasses to reuse code and behavior from parent classes.

questions.question: Does private inheritance exist in Java?
questions.explanation: Java only supports public inheritance (the 'extends' keyword).

questions.question: An abstract method has:
questions.explanation: Abstract methods define the signature but not the implementation.

questions.question: Can an abstract class be instantiated?

options.e: Yes, if it has no abstract methods

questions.explanation: Abstract classes are incomplete and cannot be instantiated.

questions.question: What is an interface?

options.b: A class with all private members

questions.explanation: Interfaces define contracts of behavior that classes must implement.

questions.question: Can interfaces contain concrete methods?

options.b: Yes, using default or static methods (Java 8+)

questions.explanation: Java 8+ allows default and static methods in interfaces.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to implement an interface?
questions.explanation: Classes implement interfaces.

questions.question: Are interface methods public by default?
questions.explanation: Methods in interfaces are implicitly public and abstract.

questions.question: Abstraction is primarily about:
questions.explanation: Abstraction simplifies complex systems by showing only necessary features.

questions.question: Can an interface extend another interface?

options.d: Only if it is a class

questions.explanation: Interfaces can extend multiple interfaces.

questions.question: Can a class implement multiple interfaces?
questions.explanation: Java supports implementing multiple interfaces.

questions.question: Are fields in interfaces implicitly:
questions.explanation: Interface variables are implicitly constants.

questions.question: Which of these is 'pure' abstraction?
questions.explanation: Interfaces represent a pure abstraction contract.

questions.question: Can an abstract class implement an interface?

options.d: Only if it has no abstract methods

questions.explanation: Yes, abstract classes can implement interfaces.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'abstract' keyword on a class?
questions.explanation: Abstract classes serve as base blueprints and cannot be directly instantiated.

questions.question: Can an abstract method be private?
questions.explanation: Abstract methods must be implemented, which is impossible if they are private/invisible.

questions.question: How do interfaces facilitate decoupling?

options.b: They define what to do, not how to do it

questions.explanation: Interfaces provide a contract; the implementing class handles the 'how'.

questions.question: What is a 'default' method in an interface?
questions.explanation: Default methods allow adding new functionality to interfaces without breaking existing implementations.

questions.question: Which of these best describes an Interface?
questions.explanation: An interface is a contract.

questions.question: Can you instantiate an anonymous interface implementation?
questions.explanation: Yes, using anonymous inner classes.

questions.question: Can interfaces have constructors?
questions.explanation: Interfaces cannot have constructors because they cannot be instantiated.

questions.question: Is it possible to have an empty interface?
questions.explanation: Marker interfaces (like Serializable) are common in Java.

questions.question: What handles automatic memory management in Java?
questions.explanation: The Garbage Collector (GC) tracks live objects and reclaims memory from unreachable ones.

questions.question: What is the scope of a static variable?
questions.explanation: Static variables are class-level variables.

questions.question: Which of these is a keyword used for constant definition?
questions.explanation: Final variables are effectively constants.

questions.question: Can static methods access instance variables?
questions.explanation: Static methods cannot access instance members directly because they exist independently of objects.

questions.question: What is a 'shallow copy' of an object?
questions.explanation: A shallow copy copies the object references.

questions.question: Which of these is a class in java.lang?
questions.explanation: These are core classes in the java.lang package.

questions.question: What does the 'transient' keyword do?
questions.explanation: Transient fields are ignored during object serialization.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a wrapper class?
questions.explanation: String is a class, not a wrapper for a primitive.

questions.question: How are primitive types different from wrapper classes?
questions.explanation: Wrappers wrap primitives into objects.

questions.question: What is 'boxing' in Java?
questions.explanation: Autoboxing converts primitives (int) to wrappers (Integer).

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'import'?
questions.explanation: Imports make classes from other packages accessible.

questions.question: Can you have multiple public classes in one file?
questions.explanation: Only one public class is allowed per .java file.

questions.question: Which of these manages object lifecycle?
questions.explanation: The GC manages the object's life from allocation to disposal.

questions.question: What happens when an object has no references?

options.b: It is eligible for Garbage Collection

questions.explanation: Unreferenced objects become garbage.

questions.question: What is the 'synchronized' keyword for?
questions.explanation: Synchronized blocks prevent race conditions in multi-threading.

questions.question: Which of these is a string comparison method?
questions.explanation: .equals() compares content, == compares references.

questions.question: What is an enum?

options.a: A special class representing a group of constants

questions.explanation: Enums simplify handling collections of related constants.

questions.question: What does the 'break' statement do?
questions.explanation: Break exits loops or switch blocks immediately.

questions.question: What is the size of a boolean in Java?
questions.explanation: The JVM specification does not mandate the size of a boolean; it is implementation-dependent.

questions.question: Which method is called right before an object is garbage collected?
questions.explanation: The finalize() method is called by the GC, though it is deprecated as of Java 9.

questions.question: Which code correctly defines a Student class with name and age fields, a constructor, and a getter for name?

options.a: public class Student { private String name; private int age; public Student(String name, int age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; } public String getName() { return name; } }
options.b: public class Student { public String name; public int age; public void Student(String n, int a) { name = n; age = a; } public String getName() { return name; } }
options.c: class student { String name; int age; student(String n, int a) { name = n; age = a; } String getName() { name; } }
options.d: public class Student { private String name; private int age; public Student() { } public String getName() { return name; } }
options.e: public class Student { String name; int age; Student(String name, int age) { name = name; age = age; } String getName() { return name; } }

questions.explanation: Option a correctly uses private fields, a parameterized constructor with this.name to resolve shadowing, and a public getter method with a proper return.

questions.question: What is wrong with this Car class implementation?

options.b: speed method should return void but returns int
options.c: The fields should be private for proper encapsulation
options.d: The class cannot have both a field and method named speed
options.e: Nothing is wrong; the code compiles and runs fine

questions.explanation: The Car class exposes its fields directly (public), breaking encapsulation. Fields should be private with getter/setter methods for controlled access.

questions.question: Which code correctly implements a BankAccount class with deposit and withdraw methods, ensuring balance never goes negative?

options.a: public class BankAccount { private double balance; public void deposit(double amt) { balance += amt; } public void withdraw(double amt) { balance -= amt; } }
options.b: public class BankAccount { private double balance; public void deposit(double amt) { if(amt>0) balance += amt; } public boolean withdraw(double amt) { if(amt>0 && amt<=balance) { balance -= amt; return true; } return false; } public double getBalance() { return balance; } }
options.c: public class BankAccount { public double balance; public void deposit(double amt) { balance = balance + amt; } public void withdraw(double amt) { if(balance >= amt) balance = balance - amt; } }
options.d: public class BankAccount { private double balance; public void add(double amt) { balance += amt; } public void take(double amt) { if(amt <= balance) balance -= amt; } }
options.e: public class BankAccount { private double balance; public void deposit(double amt) { balance += amt; } public void withdraw(double amt) throws Exception { if(amt > balance) throw new Exception(); balance -= amt; } }

questions.explanation: Option b is the most robust: it validates deposits (positive amount), returns a boolean from withdraw to indicate success, checks for sufficient funds, and encapsulates the balance field.

questions.question: Given the following code, what will be the output when main runs?

options.d: Compilation error: Dog does not override eat()

questions.explanation: Animal ref = new Dog() demonstrates polymorphism. The overridden eat() in Dog is called at runtime (dynamic binding), so ref.eat() executes Dog's version. The output is "Animal eats" (from the explicit super call) then "Dog eats bones".

questions.question: What is the output of this code involving method overloading?
questions.explanation: The first call add(5, 10) matches add(int, int) -> prints 5. The second call add(5.0, 5.5) matches add(double, double) -> prints 10.5. Java selects the most specific overload at compile-time.

questions.question: What design principle does this code violate?
questions.explanation: The fields are public, allowing direct external modification. This violates encapsulation (data hiding). Any class can set accountNumber or balance to invalid values without validation.

questions.question: Which code correctly demonstrates the use of an interface in Java?

options.a: interface Drawable { void draw(); } class Circle implements Drawable { public void draw() { System.out.println("Circle"); } }
options.b: interface Drawable { public void draw() { } } class Circle implements Drawable { void draw() { } }
options.c: interface Drawable implements Circle { void draw(); }
options.d: class Drawable { abstract void draw(); } class Circle extends Drawable { void draw() { } }
options.e: interface Drawable { abstract void draw(); } class Circle extends Drawable { void draw() { } }

questions.explanation: Option a correctly declares an interface with an abstract method, uses "implements" keyword, and provides the method implementation with public access (required when implementing interface methods).

questions.question: What is the output of this program?

options.c: Compilation error: cannot instantiate abstract class

questions.explanation: Shape is an abstract class and cannot be instantiated directly with "new Shape()". You must create a concrete subclass (like Circle) that implements the abstract method area().

questions.question: Which code correctly implements a Java class that counts how many objects have been created?

options.a: public class Counter { private static int count = 0; public Counter() { count++; } public static int getCount() { return count; } }
options.b: public class Counter { private int count = 0; public Counter() { count++; } public int getCount() { return count; } }
options.c: public class Counter { public static int count; public Counter() { this.count++; } }
options.d: public class Counter { private final int count = 0; public Counter() { count++; } public int getCount() { return count; } }
options.e: public class Counter { private static int count; public void Counter() { count++; } public int getCount() { return count; } }

questions.explanation: A static variable belongs to the class, not instances. Each constructor call increments the same count variable. A static getter returns the shared count. Option b uses an instance variable (each object has its own count starting at 0).

questions.question: What does this code print?

options.d:
Hello from Parent
Hello from Child

questions.explanation: Although the reference type is Parent, the object is a Child instance. Since greet() is overridden, Java uses dynamic binding at runtime to call Child's version of greet(). This is runtime polymorphism.

questions.question: Which is the correct way to write a Java program that reads integers from the user until they enter -1, then prints the sum?

options.a: Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); int sum = 0; int n = 0; while(n != -1) { n = sc.nextInt(); if(n != -1) sum += n; } System.out.println(sum);
options.b: Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); int sum = 0; for(int i=0; i<100; i++) { sum += sc.nextInt(); } System.out.println(sum);
options.c: int sum = 0; int n; do { n = readInt(); sum += n; } while(n != -1); print(sum);
options.d: Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); int sum = 0; while(sc.hasNext()) { sum += sc.nextInt(); } System.out.println(sum);
options.e: Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); int sum = 0; int n = sc.nextInt(); while(n != -1) { sum += n; } System.out.println(sum);

questions.explanation: Option a correctly uses a sentinel-controlled while loop: it reads input inside the loop, checks for -1 before adding to sum, and prints the total after the loop terminates.

questions.question: Which code correctly demonstrates exception handling with try-catch-finally?

options.a: try { int x = 5 / 0; } catch (Exception e) { System.out.println("Error"); } finally { System.out.println("Done"); }
options.c: try { int x = 5 / 0; } finally { System.out.println("Done"); } catch (Exception e) { }
options.e: try int x = 5 / 0; catch (Exception e) { } finally { }

questions.explanation: Option a has the correct syntax: try block, catch block with exception type, and finally block (always executes). Options b-e have syntax errors: missing catch type, wrong order, missing parentheses, or missing braces.

questions.question: What is the output of this ArrayList code?
questions.explanation: After adding 10, 20, 30, the list is [10, 20, 30]. list.remove(1) removes the element at index 1 (20), leaving [10, 30]. The size is 2.

questions.question: Which code correctly implements an inheritance hierarchy where Bird extends Animal, and both have sound methods?

options.a: class Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("Generic"); } } class Bird extends Animal { void sound() { System.out.println("Chirp"); } }
options.b: class Animal { void sound() { } } class Bird implements Animal { void sound() { } }
options.c: class Animal extends Bird { void sound() { } }
options.d: class Animal { abstract void sound(); } class Bird implements Animal { void sound() { } }
options.e: class Animal { void sound() { } } class Bird extends Animal { void sound(String s) { } }

questions.explanation: Option a correctly uses extends for inheritance, and Bird overrides sound() with its own implementation. Option b wrongly uses implements (reserved for interfaces). Option c has the hierarchy backwards. Option d uses implements with a class. Option e overloads rather than overrides (different parameter).

questions.question: What will this code print about string comparison?
questions.explanation: s1 == s2 compares references — s1 and s2 are different objects, so it's false. s1.equals(s2) compares the actual string values — both are "Hello", so it's true.

questions.question: Which code correctly creates a simple Java GUI with a button that shows a message when clicked?

options.a: JFrame frame = new JFrame(); JButton btn = new JButton("Click"); btn.addActionListener(e -> JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(null, "Hi")); frame.add(btn); frame.setVisible(true);
options.b: JFrame frame = new JFrame(); JButton btn = new JButton("Click"); btn.onClick() { System.out.println("Hi"); } frame.add(btn);
options.c: Frame f = new Frame(); Button b = new Button("Click"); b.addActionListener(this); f.add(b); f.show();
options.d: JFrame frame = new JFrame(); JButton btn = new JButton("Click"); btn.setActionCommand("Hi"); frame.add(btn); frame.show();
options.e: JFrame frame = JFrame(); JButton btn("Click"); btn.addActionListener(() -> showMessage("Hi")); frame.add(btn);

questions.explanation: Option a correctly creates a JFrame, creates a JButton with a lambda ActionListener that shows a JOptionPane, adds the button to the frame, and makes it visible.

questions.question: What is the output of this 2D array code?
questions.explanation: The code sums every element using nested for-each loops. Sum = 1+2+3+4+5+6 = 21.

questions.question: Which code correctly demonstrates the use of the final keyword with a class, method, and variable?

options.a: final class A { final void show() { final int x = 10; x = 20; } }
options.b: final class A { final void show() { final int x = 10; System.out.println(x); } } class B extends A { void show() { } }
options.c: final class A { final void show() { final int x = 10; System.out.println(x); } }
options.d: class A { final void show() { } } final class B extends A { void show() { } }
options.e: final class A { void show() { final int x; x = 10; x = 20; } }

questions.explanation: Option c correctly uses final on a class (cannot be extended), final on a method (cannot be overridden), and final on a variable (cannot be reassigned — x = 20 would cause an error). Option a tries to reassign x. Options b and d try to override a final method.

questions.question: What is the output of this code demonstrating static vs instance context?

options.d: Compilation error: cannot call instance method from static context

questions.explanation: The main method is static. Calling showInstance() directly from a static context without an object reference causes a compilation error. You must create an instance first: new Test().showInstance().

questions.question: Which code correctly implements a constructor that calls another constructor in the same class (constructor chaining)?

options.a: public class Person { String name; int age; public Person() { this("Unknown", 0); } public Person(String name, int age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; } }
options.b: public class Person { String name; int age; public Person() { Person("Unknown", 0); } public Person(String name, int age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; } }
options.c: public class Person { String name; int age; public Person() { super("Unknown", 0); } public Person(String name, int age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; } }
options.d: public class Person { String name; int age; public Person() { this("Unknown", 0); } public Person(String name, int age) { Person(name, age); } }
options.e: public class Person { String name; int age; public Person() { new Person("Unknown", 0); } public Person(String name, int age) { this.name = name; this.age = age; } }

questions.explanation: Option a correctly uses this("Unknown", 0) to call the parameterized constructor from the no-arg constructor. this() must be the first statement in the constructor. Option e creates a new object instead of chaining.
